meta.title: Disaster First Aid Decision Database
meta.description: Structured first aid database for triage based on patient age, gender, symptoms and conditions

step_3.question: Is the patient unconscious?
step_3.note: If YES, this overrides all other conditions — see unconscious protocol immediately.

unconscious.label: Unconscious Patient
steps: Check area safety: no fire, traffic, electricity, or falling objects.
Tap both shoulders and shout: 'Are you okay?' — No movement, no response = unconscious.
IMMEDIATELY call 108 (India emergency) or send someone to call.
Tilt head back, lift chin. Look, listen, feel for breathing for 5–10 seconds.
If NOT breathing or only gasping: Start CPR immediately if trained.
If breathing normally: Check pulse at wrist (radial) or neck (carotid) for up to 10 seconds.
If NO pulse: Start CPR immediately.
If pulse present: Place in recovery position (on side), keep airway open.
Loosen tight clothing (collar, belt).
If history of heart disease: Help take aspirin (160–325 mg chewed) if not unconscious and no allergy.
Keep monitoring until emergency services arrive.
Do NOT give anything by mouth to an unconscious person.
Do NOT give painkillers directly — call for specialist help.

medicines.note: NO oral medicines for unconscious patients.

specialist_only.name: Glucose (IV or nasal)
specialist_only.use: If unconscious due to very low blood sugar

specialist_only.name: Antibiotics / Antivirals
specialist_only.use: If unconscious from brain infection / meningitis

unconscious.emergency_action: CALL 108 IMMEDIATELY. No food, no water, no oral medication.

mild.label: Mild / Low-Risk Bleeding
steps: Wash hands thoroughly before touching the wound.
Clean wound with clean water or saline solution.
Apply gentle pressure with clean cloth or gauze for 5–10 minutes — do not lift to check.
Once bleeding stops, cover with sterile bandage.
Watch for signs of infection: redness, swelling, or pus.
No emergency needed unless bleeding repeatedly restarts or wound was dirty.

moderate.label: Moderate / Medium-Risk Bleeding
steps: Apply firm, continuous pressure with clean cloth or gauze.
If blood soaks through, add MORE cloth on top — do NOT remove the original.
If wound is on arm or leg, raise limb above heart level to slow bleeding.
Do NOT remove dressing to check — disturbing clot restarts bleeding.
Seek urgent medical care if bleeding does not slow after 10–15 minutes.
Go to emergency room if wound is deep, wide, or very dirty.

heavy.label: Heavy / Severe Bleeding — EMERGENCY
steps: CALL 108 IMMEDIATELY or send someone to call.
Lay person flat (unless breathing is difficult), keep them warm, reassure them.
Apply direct, strong pressure over bleeding site with clean cloth or towel.
If soaked, add more cloth ON TOP — keep pressing.
For major limb bleeding not stopping with pressure: apply tourniquet HIGH on limb (only if trained).
Do NOT remove embedded objects (knife, glass) — stabilize with padding and press AROUND it.
Monitor for shock: pale skin, rapid pulse, confusion.

medicines.name: No oral pain killers until bleeding is controlled
medicines.note: Do not give medicines until emergency team arrives for heavy bleeding

medicines.name: Betadine
medicines.use: After bleeding is controlled, for antiseptic

fractures.label: Fractures
general_steps: Stop any bleeding with pressure using a clean cloth.
Apply ice wrapped in cloth for 15–20 minutes to reduce swelling.
Immobilize the area ABOVE and BELOW the fracture with splints, bandages, or slings.
Keep the person still and treat for shock if needed.
Monitor circulation every 15 minutes (check if fingers/toes are warm and pink).
Do NOT move the patient if head, neck, or back injury is involved.

hand.label: Hand Fracture
steps: Support hand in a raised sling against the body.
Pad gently and secure with broad bandages.
Avoid using the hand.
Elevate hand to minimize swelling until help arrives.

leg.label: Leg Fracture
steps: Splint the injured leg to the uninjured one using triangular bandages or padding.
Immobilize the ankle and foot.
Do NOT raise the injured leg if shock is present.
Keep the person lying down and still.

spinal.label: Spinal Fracture
steps: Do NOT move the patient at all — spinal movement can cause permanent paralysis.
Call 108 immediately.
Keep the person lying flat and still.
Support the head and neck in current position — do not try to straighten.
If breathing stops, only then carefully open airway with jaw thrust, not head tilt.
Keep patient calm and reassured until help arrives.

medicines.name: Paracetamol (Crocin/Calpol)
medicines.use: Mild to moderate pain — safest first line

medicines.name: Ibuprofen (Brufen/Combiflam)
medicines.use: Pain and swelling — avoid if stomach ulcers or kidney issues
medicines.warning: Concerns with long-term use for bone healing — use short-term only

medicines.name: Volini gel
medicines.use: Apply topically 3x/day for local pain relief

fever.label: Fever
fever.definition: Temporary rise in body temperature above 100.4°F / 38°C
steps: Rest in a cool, well-ventilated room.
Drink plenty of fluids: water, ORS, herbal teas (ginger / tulsi).
Use lukewarm sponging or compresses on forehead and armpits.
Wear light clothing — do NOT bundle up.
Seek medical help if fever exceeds 103°F or lasts more than 3 days.

medicines.name: Paracetamol (Crocin/Calpol)
medicines.use: Reduce fever and pain — safest option

medicines.name: Ibuprofen (Brufen)
medicines.use: Fever and inflammation — avoid in children under 6 months

nausea.label: Nausea
steps: Sip ORS, clear broths, or electrolyte drinks in small, frequent amounts.
Rest completely.
Eat bland foods (rice, toast, bananas) only once nausea settles.
Wash hands frequently to prevent spreading.
Avoid strong smells, spicy or fatty food.

suffocation.label: Suffocation / Claustrophobia
steps: Move person to open, well-ventilated space immediately.
Open windows and use a fan to increase airflow.
Loosen tight clothing (collar, belt).
Practice 4-7-8 breathing: inhale 4s, hold 7s, exhale 8s.
Box breathing alternative: inhale 4s, hold 4s, exhale 4s.
Sip cold water slowly.
Distract with calm visualization or conversation.
Call 108 if breathing does not improve within minutes.

medicines.name: Lorazepam
medicines.warning: Do not administer without medical advice

anxiety.label: Anxiety / Panic
steps: Move to a calm, quiet space.
Practice 4-7-8 breathing or box breathing.
Speak calmly and reassuringly to the person.
Ground technique: name 5 things you see, 4 you touch, 3 you hear.
Avoid caffeine and stimulants.
Do NOT leave the person alone during a panic attack.

vomiting.label: Vomiting
steps: Sip ORS or ginger tea in small amounts — 1–2 tablespoons every 15 minutes.
Rest with head elevated.
Avoid solid food until vomiting settles.
Try acupressure: press P6 point on wrist (inner wrist, 3 fingers from crease).
Chew fennel seeds (saunf) to settle stomach.
Seek help if blood in vomit or vomiting lasts more than 24 hours.

medicines.name: Eldoper (Loperamide)
medicines.use: If diarrhea present — avoid if blood in stool

severe_cold.label: Severe Cold
steps: Inhale steam with a bowl of hot water — add a few drops of eucalyptus oil.
Gargle with warm salt water (1 tsp salt in warm water) 2–3 times daily.
Drink hot turmeric milk (haldi doodh) before bed.
Apply honey-ginger mix for throat relief.
Stay well hydrated.
Elevate head while sleeping to ease congestion.
Rest in a warm room.

headache.label: Headache
steps: Apply cold compress to forehead for 10–15 minutes.
Drink at least 2 litres of water — dehydration is a common cause.
Massage temples gently in circular motion.
Rest in a dark, quiet room.
Apply peppermint oil to temples.
Sip black tea (caffeine can relieve tension headaches).
Try gentle neck stretches.

trauma.label: Trauma / Injury / Shock
steps: Stop bleeding with clean cloth — apply firm pressure.
If fracture suspected: immobilize the area; do not move.
Elevate injured limb if no spinal concern.
Treat for shock: lay person flat, elevate legs (if conscious and no spinal injury).
Apply RICE method: Rest, Ice (10–15 min), Compression bandage, Elevate.
Reassure the person calmly — shock worsens with panic.
Call 108 for serious trauma.

allergy.label: Allergic Reaction
steps: Remove or move away from the allergen immediately.
Apply cool compress on rash or affected skin.
Take antihistamine immediately.
Avoid rubbing or scratching the affected area.
Apply cold oatmeal paste on rash for relief.
For severe reaction (throat swelling, difficulty breathing): CALL 108 IMMEDIATELY — this is anaphylaxis.

burn.label: Burns
steps: Immediately cool burn under running lukewarm water for 20 minutes.
Do NOT use ice, butter, toothpaste, or oil — these worsen burns.
Cover loosely with a clean, non-fluffy cloth or sterile gauze.
Do not burst blisters.
Apply aloe vera gel (fresh leaf preferred) after cooling.
For large burns or burns on face/hands/genitals: CALL 108.

carbon_monoxide.label: Carbon Monoxide Inhalation
steps: Move person to FRESH AIR IMMEDIATELY — outdoors or open windows.
Call 108 immediately.
If unconscious and not breathing: start CPR if trained.
Loosen clothing.
Do NOT go back into the contaminated area.
Keep person lying down and still.
Hyperventilate in fresh air if conscious.
Oxygen therapy is required — cannot be administered at home.

medicines.name: NO OTC medicines available for CO poisoning
medicines.note: Pure oxygen is the only treatment — administered by emergency services only

carbon_monoxide.emergency_action: CALL 108. Get to fresh air. Oxygen therapy required.

high_bp.label: High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
steps: Sit or lie down quietly in a calm environment.
Take slow, deep breaths.
Avoid salt, caffeine, or any stimulants.
Loosen tight clothing.
Monitor BP if possible.
Do NOT exert — rest completely.
Seek medical care if BP remains very high or if symptoms include chest pain, vision changes, or severe headache.

low_bp.label: Low Blood Pressure (Hypotension)
steps: Lay person down flat and raise their legs above heart level.
Provide fluids: water, ORS, or salty snacks.
Avoid sudden standing.
Keep the person warm.
If unconscious due to low BP: CALL 108 immediately.
Monitor pulse regularly.

medicines.name: Shelcal (Calcium supplement)
medicines.use: If low calcium contributing to low BP

diabetes.label: Diabetic Emergency (Hypoglycemia / Low Sugar)
steps: If conscious: give glucose drink, Glucon-D, or sugar immediately.
Give candy, fruit juice, or glucose biscuits.
If unconscious: DO NOT give anything by mouth — CALL 108.
Monitor consciousness closely.
Check blood sugar if glucometer available.
Keep person lying down and calm.
Recheck sugar level after 15 minutes.

medicines.name: GlucaGen injection
medicines.use: For severe unconscious hypoglycemia — PRESCRIPTION / PROFESSIONAL ONLY

diabetes.emergency_action: If unconscious: CALL 108. Do NOT give oral glucose.

age_medicine_warnings.infant: No OTC medicines without paediatrician advice. Most adult medicines are contraindicated.
age_medicine_warnings.child: Paediatric doses apply. Ibuprofen not for under 6 months. Always halve or quarter adult doses unless labelled for children.
age_medicine_warnings.elderly: Reduce doses. Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen) if possible — risk of kidney/stomach issues. Monitor closely.
age_medicine_warnings.teen: Use lower range of adult doses. Avoid aspirin for viral illnesses (Reye's syndrome risk).

female.note: Heavy menstrual bleeding with dizziness may require urgent care — treat as moderate-to-heavy bleeding. Pregnancy: avoid ibuprofen, especially in 3rd trimester.

male.note: No specific gender-based differences for listed conditions.
